segments.text:
Chapter 15
Cardiovascular system

Chuck Norris
Chuck Norris once kicked a horse in the chin. Its descendants are known today as Giraffes.

How blood travels through the body
Heart         Arteries          arterioles
Capillaries          venules            veins
Heart
Anatomy and physiology revealed:
Video – Cardiovascular system overview

Heart
The heart is a hollow, cone-shaped, muscular bag that pumps blood to our bodies tissues.
4 chambers: 2 receiving atria, 2 pumping ventricles
Of course the heart size per individual will vary since it needs to supply the metabolic demand of the body…big body = big demand but on average: The heart is approximately 14 cm long, 9cm wide.

Heart location
The heart sits in the mediastinum of the thorax (in the axial portion above the diaphragm) of the body. Anatomically it is:
posterior to the sternum
medial to the lungs
anterior to the vertebral column
the base (top of the heart) lies beneath the
the second rib; the apex (pointed bottom) is at the 5 th intercostal space, points to the left, and lays just above the diaphragm.

Two circuits
Blood leaves the heart going in two simultaneous directions
Leaving the Right Ventricle is deoxygenated blood heading to pick up O2 and dump off CO2 in the lungs. This is called pulmonic circulation .
Similarly, leaving the left ventricle is oxygenated blood heading for the body and the heart itself. This is called systemic circulation .
Anatomy and Physiology revealed: Video
Pulmonary and systemic circulation

Pericardium
The pericardium is aka the pericardial sac and encloses and protects the heart and large proximal vessels.
Layers : outer         inner
(supf)         (deep)
Fibrous pericardium    	Parietal pericardium   	Visceral pericardium
(aka epicardium)
( Between these two layers is the pericardial cavity containing serous fluid to decrease friction)
*the parietal pericardium is a continuation of visceral pericardium turned inside-out on itself*
Pericardium
Pericarditis

Wall of the heart:
The wall of the heart is composed of 3 distinct layers: an outer epicardium, a middle myocardium, and an inner endocardium
Epicardium (visceral pericardium)- made up of connective tissue covered by epithelium; protects heart by reducing friction (serous membrane producing serous fluid)
Myocardium- muscular layer richly supplied with blood capillaries, lymphatic capillaries, and nerve fibers. (remember: myo=muscle)
Endocardium- lines all the heart chambers; made up of epithelium on top of connective tissue; contains the purkinje fibers used in muscle cell conductivity

Heart Anatomy
Again, the heart is divided into four chambers:
Two Atria- each of which have ear-like projections coming off of them anteriorly to increase overall volume.
Two Ventricles- a lower pressure rt side and higher pressure left side.
Atria hold the blood returning to the heart from systemic and pulmonic circulation.
Ventricles are much larger and pump the blood back into systemic and pulmonic circulation.
Interiorly we have an interatrial septum that separate atria from each other and an interventricular septum that separate the ventricles from each other. (if someone suffers from a interatrial septal defect, then we have a hole or window between the two atria)
Several large grooves encircle the outer portion of the heart. The groove separating the atria from the ventricles is called the coronary (or AV) sulcus . The two grooves (ant and post) separating the ventricles are appropriately called the interventricular sulci .

Major heart structures
Valves:
Between each atria and its corresponding ventricle is an orifice protected by a valve. These valves prevent backflow when the heart is pumping.
On the right side we have the tricuspid valve and on the left we have the bicuspid valve (aka mitral valve)  	*LAB RAT*
Both valves are referred to as AV valves or atrioventricular valves
*In order to make sure we are not having the valve turn the wrong way we also have chordae tendineae (acting like parachute cords) attached to papillary mm . Contraction of papillary mm occurs at the same time as ventricular contraction…insuring tightly closed valves.

(aka bicuspid)
Tricuspid valve
Mitral valve
Chordae tendineae
Papillary mm

Major heart structures
Just as we have valves between the atria and ventricles we also have valves between the ventricles and their destinations.
Coming between the right atrium and the pulmonic trunk we have the pulmonic valve and between the left ventricle and the aortic arch is the aortic valve .
*These two valves may also be called semilunar valves*

Pulmonic valve
Aortic valve

Blood Circulation
Old blood comes first.
Rt atrium is our starting point- so we have to fill it with low O2, high CO2 blood from the body (and heart itself) aka blood from the systemic circulation.
This blood has traveled through the veins and enters the right atrium via the superior and inferior vena cava as well as the coronary sinus.
- Superior vena cava brings blood back from upper body (head, neck, arms…brachiocephalic circ)
- Inferior vena cava brings blood back from lower body (GI, legs, reproductive organs, kidneys)
- Coronary sinus brings back blood from the coronary circulation around the heart (vessels that supply the heart tissue itself)

Blood circulation
So…now that we have received our used blood back from the body and heart we need to re-oxygenate that blood, but first we need to move into the pump.
Rt. Atrium       Rt. Ventricle (ventricles have the pumping power) receives blood from the Rt. Atrium via the tricuspid valve. *RAT*
Once rt ventricle is filled, it pumps blood into the lungs (pulmonic circulation) to dump off the CO2 and pick up more O2.
Rt. Ventricle pumps this blood via the pulmonic (semilunar) valve to the pulmonary trunk which splits into pulmonary aa, eventually carrying blood to the alveolar capillaries which exchange gases with the alveoli on the lungs…once blood filters through the lungs, and diffusion of gases occur, the O2 rich blood returns to the heart through the pulmonary veins.

*Only veins that carry O2 rich blood*
Blue = O2 depleted blood
Red = O2 rich blood
Why do we always bleed red?

Blood Circulation
New blood:
So…now we have O2 rich blood returning from the lungs back into the Left atrium (atriums store blood).
Blood now needs to go back out to the body to deliver the oxygen (systemic circulation)
So how do we pump things?   Through ventricles
Blood moves from the left atrium into the left ventricle via the mitral valve (bicuspid) 	 *LAB*
When the ventricle is full, it contracts, pushing blood into the Aorta by passing through the aortic (semiluar) valve.

Blood supply to the heart
Again, as we mentioned earlier, coronary (heart) circulation is part of systemic circulation.
When coronary circulation is restricted (angina) or stopped (MI) we have big problems.

Coronary circulation
The first two branches of the aorta, called the right and left coronary arteries , supply the tissues of the heart. Their openings lie just beyond the aortic valve.
The right coronary artery : passes along the coronary sulcus b/w the rt atrium and the right ventricle. Yields two major branches- posterior interventricular artery (supplies walls of both ventricles posteriorly ) and marginal artery (supplies rt atrium and rt ventricle)
The left coronary artery : yields two major branches- the circumflex artery (supplies blood to walls of left atrium and left ventricle) and the anterior interventricular artery (a.k.a. the left anterior descending artery; supplies both ventricles anteriorly )
Amongst these major arteries their will also be smaller vessels interconnecting them. These interconnections are called anastomoses and provide collateral circulation (circulation that can come from more than one point in case a vessel is blocked).
Blood drains through cardiac veins which run parallel to the arteries and the veins join to form the coronary sinus on the back of the heart.

anterior view
left coronary artery
left anterior interventricular artery (left anterior descending artery
right coronary artery

posterior view
circumflex artery
posterior interventricular artery (posterior descending artery)
coronary sinus

Atherosclerosis
Atherosclerosis is a disease of the arteries in which fatty material and plaque are deposited in the wall of an artery, resulting in narrowing of the arterial lumen and eventual impairment of blood flow.
When atherosclerosis builds up in coronary arteries we can get an MI
Blood clot and atherosclerosis

Cardiac Cycle
Systole and Diastole : 120/80
Systole = contraction phase (atria and ventricles have a systole)
Diastole = relaxation/refilling phase

Cardiac cycle
Atrial systole occurs in order to fill the ventricles up with the last bit of blood remaining in the left and rt atrium.
Ventricles usually fill automatically, up to about 70%
Atrial systole fills that last 30%

Cardiac Cycle
Blood fills the ventricles and the mmHg in the ventricles rises . When the muscular walls of the ventricles contract we have ventricular systole . Increased pressure in the ventricles and contraction of the papillary mm on the chordae tendineae both insure A-V valve closure. At the same time atrial pressure is now low (lower than venous pressure) and the atria can fill with returning blood as the ventricles are getting ready to eject.
Aortic and pulmonic valves are forced open as heart pressure rises to overcome the pulmonic and systemic resistance.
Once mmHg is greater in the heart than the vessels they are pumping into, blood leaves the ventricles. Then heart mmHg falls back down below peripheral resistance (pulmonary aa and aorta) the aortic and pulmonic valves are forced shut again… the heart then relaxes and the ventricles can fill up with blood again (since atria were already filling their pressure will exceed ventricular chamber pressure opening AV valves again).

Cardiac Cycle : Valves closing
During the relaxed phase (diastole) the aortic and pulmonic valves have closed so the heart can fill again (peripheral resistance forces them shut)
During Ventricular contraction (ventricular systole) the back mmHg from the blood getting squeezed in the ventricles forces the tricuspid and mitral valves shut (av valves)

Contractions
Relaxation/filling phase

Heart Sounds
1 st heart sound / S1 (lubb) – AV valves slamming shut as the ventricles contract to pump blood
…occurs during ventricular systole
2 nd heart sound / S2 (dubb) – aortic and pulmonic valves slam shut as peripheral resistance becomes > heart mmHg
….occurs during ventricular diastole

Abnormal heart sounds
Murmurs
Diastolic murmurs = arms and parts or ARMS PRTS
…During diastole the ventricles are refilling so AV valves should be open (if stenosis occurs = turbulent blood flow) while the pulmonic and aortic should be closed (unless they are regurgitating)
Systolic murmurs = aortic or pulmonic stenosis / mitral or tricuspid regurgitation
Murmurs are typically due to problems with the valves closing (regurgitant murmurs) or by difficulty of blood passing through a narrowed opening (stenotic murmurs).
AV valves

Where do we auscultate for heart sounds?
A PET Monkey
At the aortic, pulmonic valves, erb’s point (best place to auscultate S2), and the tricuspid and mitral valves.

Conducting a heartbeat signal
Cardiac muscle fibers fxn similarly to skeletal muscle fibers but remember that the fibers connect in branching networks . Stimulation to any part of the network sends impulses throughout the heart.
Throughout the heart are clumps and strands of specialized cardiac muscle tissue whose fibers are not their to necessarily contract, but rather send signals. They comprise the cardiac conduction system .
Functional Syncytium – fibers of muscle that conduct/contract at the same time; a mass of merging cells that act as a single unit.
Two fxnal syncytium in the heart = atrial syncytium and ventricular syncytium .
The cardiac conduction system coordinates the events of the cardiac cycle
The signal starts at the pacemaker of the heart which is the SA node or sinoatrial node. This signal is usually spontaneously generated at about 80x per minute in the adult but typically slowed down by the vagus nerve to 70bpm.

Conduction pathway
SA node- small, elongated mass of specialized cardiac muscle tissue it is in the right atrium near the opening of the sup 		vena cava. Its fibers are continous with the atrial syncytium. Reaches threshold spontaneously
in response to mv’mt of Ca++, Na+, and K+ ion concentrations
Atrial syncytium- mass of merging cells that act together in the atrium; starts with internodal atrial muscle which carry signals
to more distant areas of the atrium then the gap jxns (connections between m fibers) spread the signal around 		so the atria can contract together at virually the same time.
Junctional fibers- fibers of the conduction system which are continuous with atrial muscle fibers
AV node (atrioventricular)- located in the inf portion of the interatrial septum;  slowly receives signals from 		junctional fibers and transmits the signal inferiorly along to the AV bundle. (this is necessary because
the signal cannot pass through the fibrous skeleton of the heart p. 560)
AV bundle (of His)- larger fibers that transmit the signal more rapidly starting at the upper part on the 				interventricular septum
Bundle branches- continue down the rt and left portion of the septum and give rise to the purkinje fibers.
Purkinje fibers- large fibers that carry impulses to distant portions of the ventricles
Ventricular syncytium- ventricles contract N-SYNC
Conduction System
Conduction system
Conduction

Problems with conduction pathway
Arrythmia = not rhythmic
AV nodal blocks
Bundle branch blocks – ventricles can no longer synchronize
Pacemakers

Trace a drop of blood
Also be aware you may be asked to trace a drop of blood from one portion of the body back to systemic circulation.

End of Cardio presentation part I
